speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to introduce Stephanie Heller and her family. Stephanie, would you like to introduce the members of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, first, I will introduce my dear husband, Robert, who has suffered with me for 42 years. And here is my daughter Naomi. My son is not here. He lives in Brisbane with his family. My sister is here, who is my twin sister, and maybe because of her I am here. And my son-in-law, Ellie, my granddaughter-- first grandchild-- Julie, my second grandchild, Ariel, third one is Daniel, and then little Ariel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tal!

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry, sorry, Tal. I made a mistake.

speaker: interviewee
text: He looks just like Ariel. That's why.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, Annetta.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Robert, would you like to say a few words to Stephanie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was a big decision-- big family decision-- to allow Stephanie to participate in not only this Shoah film, but also in previous documentaries, and her commitment to the Holocaust Center to revive her memories. Because every time she talks about it, she relives it. And it is not only that she's been doing it now for the last couple of years or so, previously, other people had came to her and asking her for memories, and remind us of people she met in the camp-- in the respect of being witnesses to claims and so on. Stepha suffered a lot in her-- in her youth. I was very lucky. I didn't go through it because I lived in Africa. But Stephanie, for many, many years, has had-- has had stress symptoms from what she went through-- stomach problems, and then for about 25, nearly 30 years, she was suffering from migraines-- which all doctors attributed it only to her suffering. As I say, it was therefore a very big decision whether or not she should participate in any-- in anything, which would revive her memories, because it might trigger it again. Therefore, we admire very much what she's doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my family always say I cry at everything, but my mother's very special. My whole family is special-- my aunt's special, my mother, and my dad, too. And we just want everybody to know that even though we don't say that we understand, we do understand. And we feel for you. Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: There are no need many words to say how lucky we are that we are together with all our families. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, just a few words. Stepha, I'd like to take this opportunity to thank you for being a great help for us, helping with the kids. And I'm sure you continue helping in the future. And not having my parents here, I feel that Stepha and Robbie are like my parents. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to say, for the grandchildren, that we feel really lucky to have a grandmother like Grannie-- and Stepha-- who gives us so much. She's like no other grandparents help, like, their grandchildren as much as Grannie does. And a great aunt who is so kind as Auntie Annetta. And I wish my dad was here-- he would have loved to be here, but he's in Queensland. So that's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd just like to say how lucky I am to have a grandmother like Stepha. She's been really nice to us. And I love her a lot. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. My name is Pauline Wrobel. I am conducting an interview with Stephanie Heller on the 31st of October, 1996, in Melbourne, Australia. Can you please give me your name and spelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Stephanie Heller. Stephanie, S-T-E-P-H-A-N-I-E, Heller, H-E-L-L-E-R. My original name was Spanka. That spelling is a little bit difficult, I suppose-- S-- with a little hook-- P-A-N-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have a Hebrew name, Dinah, D-I-N-A-H. But that is which I actually made myself to bear, when I came to Israel. I wasn't born under that name.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born, Stephanie?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on 1st of February, 24.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm 72 now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born, Stephanie?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Yugoslavia, but that is not my country. I was born there because we were premature. I'm a twin, and my twin sister and I decided to arrive earlier than expected. And our mother was visiting family in Yugoslavia. So we were born in Yugoslavia, in Subotica, S-U-B-O-T-I-C-A, Yugoslovia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your address in Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I couldn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my address in Czechoslovakia? As infants, we, straight away, we came to live-- we were living in Czechoslovakia. We had originally-- we were living on Chodcka, C-H-O-D-C-K-A, in Prague 12, that was as children we lived there. And was it a house or apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who lived with you in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father, Mother, and then we had a little sister who was born six years later. Six year-- she's six years younger. And we had a maid who lived in the apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just a simple dwelling. We didn't have any great luxuries. I mean, in those times, there were no TVs and so on. But we were very, happy children. We had always good care. Our mother was a stenographist. And she was having a job at as a stenographer in a firm, textile firm. And she was working. So we had the maid who looked after us, and after the household.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the area that you've lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The area was middle class, middle-class people in Prague, Prague-- the center, more or less. It was a very good area, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall your grandparents, Stephanie?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never had any grandparents. They were dead before we were born from both sides.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: I actually could tell you about my grandfather from my mother's side, who was a doctor. And we heard a lot about him. He was a very strong-minded personality. And my mother always said that that is in the family. That's just a remark about one grandparent. My father was a-- was a-- as a guide, a tourist guide. And he was mostly out, because during the weekends and holidays, he was very often out. He was working for a big firm, [NON-ENGLISH] Company in Prague. And so mother took, usually, us girls for outings on weekends. And holidays, we went to the country.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother believed very much in fresh air and exercises. And we have been members of a group of-- of soccer that is something like commonwealth [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sports. And we have been acting in two special like olympiad-- how would I call it? We have been part of a big doing from the-- from the patriotic soccer-- like Maccabi here. Was your father a religious man?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was probably religious, but he didn't keep much to it. And I know that the high holidays, we went to the synagogues, And he was working there as a shamas, as a guide or something. So he must have been affiliated. But we haven't really felt like-- we were not really religious. We knew we are Jewish, but that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your relationship with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really very close, because he didn't have much time for us. And in the olden days, usually, in our times, the care for the children was left to the mother. We had a very, very warm and close relationship with our mother. Father was there, but Mother was always the one who has done the decisions and told us and told us her philosophy. I will never forget. She has been telling us always-- look at the brighter side. And something could be worse. We had been reading a book together, called, about a young girl whose philosophy was when she broke her leg, and she had to be in bed, she was happy because she could use her hands for knitting. And my mother, for always, told us to use this philosophy in life. And it might have been something that really helped us during our bad times. Because we always thought it could be worse. For instance, when I was in concentration camp, along with my sister, I was always feeling it's much better than it could be because I have my sister around.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Therese.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Arthur.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother a religious person?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. She believed in Judaism. She told us the commandments to obey and so on. But we didn't keep kosher household. And, we-- so I said, we only went to synagogue at high holidays. We didn't keep Shabbat either.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother work full time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, before Hitler came, she was working full time-- apart of the holidays that she took for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned your sisters. Can you tell me about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my twin sister was, we always felt-- firstly, we are identical. And as children, nobody really knew who was who. And even now, in some old pictures, we have been given-- somebody salvaged them-- we don't know which one is which. And our father had trouble in finding the culprit when something happened. And he wanted to punish us-- which one was it? He usually punished both of us because he didn't know for sure. [LAUGHS] And, of course, we have taken advantage of it. When we were at school, we had-- when I knew something better, I claimed that I'M Annetta-- MY sister's name is Annetta. A-N-N-E-T-T-A. And we felt, actually, that we are two bodies and one soul. When I dreamt something, and I told Annetta about it later in the years, she saw that it was her dream. And when I was sick, and she was not there-- I have been, as a baby, had some operation on my skull, some abscess or whatever-- AND I was operated on. And my mother told us that my sister cried all the time as if she would have had the pain. And later on, it was always mingled, what-- the feelings that my sister had in my-- instead of me. I-- we have it even now. If something bothers her, I know that. And if-- she knows when I am happy that she feels happy too. And our little sister, well, we tried to boss her. We tried to bring her up because we thought we are the old sisters. And she was a naughty little girl. She was spoiled. Father was spoiling her more than us. And she was a sweet little girl. And, unfortunately, we lost her much too early. Her name was Elizabeth. Her original name was Albeta in Czech. That's A-L-B-E-T-A. Albeta.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school, Stephanie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we went to school. We went to state school. We didn't have many Jewish children in school. I can't recollect that there would be more than us, really, Jewish children. We didn't go to Jewish groups. We mingled with population as it was. It was democratic republic, and we were patriotic. I remember that at school, in the high school, I have been chosen to parade in front of President Masaryk with the flag because I won a prize in some essay about the Czechoslovakia Republic. Something very great, you know, patriotic. And I was very proud because I have been the one to-- to walk in front and hold the flag. [LAUGHS] And my sister has made some beautiful pictures. And we presented President Masaryk with some album. I can't remember exactly details. But we were patriotic. That we were Jewish, that was A part of, you know, that was part of our life. But we didn't feel any different than the others.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you encounter anti-Semitism in your youth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in small ways. The peasants-- when we were in-- on the-- in the country during the holidays, I could hear somebody-- oh, those are the Jewish kids. But not in bad, you know, just we have been regarded as different as Jews. And we didn't know any really religious Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't take much notice of it. And then later, when Hitler started in Europe-- we had-- we have been young then-- we had kids saying, oh, you Jews. Go to Palestine. Something like that. Was that in school you heard that sort of comment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Playground, probably-- not in school. I don't-- the teachers never made any differences between us. But we have always strived-- I think it was mother's influence that she somehow made us fear that we have to be trying to be better to show that we are a good bridge-- something like that-- to show good example. We have not been allowed to do some mischief at school because we we're supposed to be the example. That's what our mother told us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember a particular memory that you shared with your mother, an experience that you shared with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, there are so many. And they are a little bit sad to think about. It was very close relationship. But we haven't been talking about it much. We just felt it. I remember one holiday, that is just a picture, which I'll never forget. We and our mother, we were somewhere in the mountain. And our little girl, little sister, was with us. And it was such a beautiful day, and we have been so happy. It's my closest memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you spend your time as a young girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we have been always very busy with school work and looking after ourselves. We were not spoilt, really. Even though that we have the girl who looked after the household and cooked and so on, we had to do our cleaning, our chores, and cleaning our room, maybe. And we have played together. And we have been taught how to sew a little dresses for the dolls. I remember that when were outside in the country on holidays, we have built little damns in the brook. We were always busy. We always-- I can't remember that we would have been ever bored because we have been always together, my sister and I. We quarreled, of course, too. She knew better. I knew better. We, maybe, sometimes even quarreled. So much that our mother had to say, come on, stop it. The maid has to separate us. But they didn't fight, really. But we were just normal children. But I could say in honesty that our childhood was a happy one. Normal, happy one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What interests did you have outside of school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this-- sports, that was gymnastics that we went to, and writing and reading. We had been writing, I don't know, poems or stories or whatever. But meeting lots of friends-- games, going out. Nothing special-- we didn't go out, like nowadays, the youngsters go. Nothing outstanding, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your relationship with the Czech community at large?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plain. I didn't feel any difference. We didn't feel that we are intruders or that we don't belong. We were part of the community. That was all. Only when Hitler came, then we were made to understand that our-- that we are not the same. That Hitler came in, there was-- he occupied Czechoslovakia, and everything was different. We have-- people lost their properties. The houses of Jewish people were occupied, and other people took over. Businesses were taking over-- the Aryanization. We had to use, to have the Star of David on our clothes with writing on it-- Jude-- a Jew. We were not allowed to go to school anymore. That was when we were in high school. We wanted to study, actually, afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date did this happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's difficult to remember the date. It was '39 when Hitler-- when war started. Hitler came in '39, I think it was March. I can't tell you exactly the day. We were told that we can't go to school anymore, and our little sister, just as well. She was being taught at private homes. Some Jewish children were tutored. But even later, it was difficult to do it because it was forbidden. And my sister and I, we have been-- we have tried to learn. My mother tried to get us out of the country. There was a joint Jewish organization who took children to England. And in effort to do that, they gave us a chance to learn to be nurses-- not nurses-- nannies, nannies. And it was a course, which was short. And we had been looking after little kids, and we were taught how to handle little kids and how to look after them. And we were 15, 16 then. And we even looked after them. And then we couldn't go to England, because our mother stipulated that we should be put together in a family together. Nobody wanted to have two or three kids together. So our waiting list was longer and longer. And then we just didn't get in. So we couldn't get out. But we didn't bother about that. We didn't worry. Then afterwards, we have been working in the homes of Jewish people who needed some nanny. My sister started to work. I have been starting to learn to do some sewing because not being able to go to school, I wanted to learn some trade. And I was apprentice to a Jewish seamstress. And I have been only starting. But then I found out that I won't be able to get the certificate, because Jews wouldn't get certificate after-- or training, which was a long, long future.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I didn't continue. And my sister was fortunate enough to obtain a job after working at some private homes as nanny, to work in their Jewish hospital, Jewish orphanage. There was in Prague, a big Jewish orphanage, run by Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were a lot of Jewish children. And Annetta got a job as a domestic there. And after a while, I have got the same job as her. So we were living in the orphanage. And we were working there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Prague. This was all in Prague. And it was-- we were quite happy there because we have met young people-- there were other young girls who were working in the orphanage. And visitors came there. Young boys came there, so guess I think like nowadays, the young people here, the teenagers, go and go to parties or go to see gather in somebody's homes. They came to the orphanage because we were not allowed to go out. We were not allowed to have, after 8 o'clock, to be outside. So they came there. Sometimes, they even slept there, you know, the young people. And we have been darning socks. We were sitting around the table. We have been given cake and something to drink. And we have been darning socks for the children. And the boys for entertaining us, maybe, playing on the guitar. Maybe we were talking, singing. It was not so bad. But it didn't take long. Our parents lived in a flat-- in the in flat we were living before. Not in the same flat, in different one. And we just visited them. But the life was very, very harsh for my parents because both of them lost their jobs. They couldn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: We will have to end. Thank you. This is the end of tape one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
